elements.content:
JavaScript Lives in the Browser

Your code runs inside a JS engine, then reaches the real world through browser-provided APIs.

The Call Stack Runs One Thing at a Time

Function calls stack up, run to completion, then pop off in reverse order.

Web APIs Do Background Work

Timers, clicks, and network requests are handed to the browser so the stack can keep moving.

Two Queues Decide What Runs Next

Promise callbacks go to microtasks. Events and timers go to the task queue.

The Event Loop Is the Traffic Cop

Run one task. Drain all microtasks. Repeat forever while the page stays alive.

Sync vs Async in 5 Lines

Synchronous code runs now. The timer callback runs later, even with a zero delay.

Clicks Become Tasks

A user event waits in the task queue until the event loop sends its handler to the stack.

Fetch Resolves as a Microtask

The network runs in the background. When data returns, the promise callback gets priority as a microtask.

JavaScript in 10 Seconds-ish

The browser keeps apps responsive by separating immediate work from background work and queued callbacks.

- Call stack: runs current JavaScript
- Web APIs: handle timers, events, and network work
- Task queue: events and timers wait here
- Microtask queue: promise callbacks run with priority
- Event loop: moves work onto the stack in the right order
